verses.commentary: Paul appeals by Christ's meekness and gentleness while addressing accusations of being timid in person but bold from a distance. He owns the criticism to reframe it Christologically.
He begs them not to force him to be bold when present toward those who think he operates by worldly standards. Boldness is available but undesired; correction should be unnecessary.
Living in the world does not mean waging war according to worldly methods. Christian existence is incarnational (in the world) but not worldly in operational strategy.
Spiritual weapons possess divine power to demolish strongholds. The warfare is real but operates in a different dimension; the weapons are not physical but divinely empowered.
Demolishing arguments and pretensions that oppose God's knowledge, taking every thought captive to obey Christ. The battle is intellectual and spiritual; minds are the contested territory.
Readiness to punish disobedience awaits completion of Corinthian obedience. Paul delays discipline until the majority submit, isolating the rebels rather than confronting while the church is divided.
They judge by appearances. If anyone is confident they belong to Christ, they should reconsider that Paul belongs to Christ equally. Surface evaluation misses spiritual reality.
Paul may boast freely about his authority, given by the Lord for building up, not tearing down. Authority has constructive purpose; legitimate boasting will not produce shame.
He doesn't want to seem to frighten them with letters. The accusation is that his letters are intimidating; Paul wants ministry based on relationship, not fear.
Critics say his letters are weighty and forceful, but his bodily presence is weak and speech amounts to nothing. The accusation exposes the contrast between written and physical Paul.
What Paul is in letters when absent, he will be in actions when present. Consistency between correspondence and conduct is his defense; no gap between word and deed.
Refusing to compare themselves with self-commenders who measure themselves by themselves. Self-referential evaluation produces folly; wisdom requires external standards.
Paul will not boast beyond proper limits but confines boasting to his assigned sphere of service, which includes Corinth. God-assigned boundaries define legitimate boasting.
They are not overextending by boasting about Corinth because they actually reached Corinth with the gospel. Geographic precedence establishes apostolic claim.
Not boasting in others' labor but hoping that as Corinthian faith grows, Paul's sphere of activity will greatly expand. Faith's increase creates space for extended ministry.

The goal is preaching the gospel in regions beyond Corinth, not boasting about work already done in someone else's territory. Pioneer mission, not appropriating others' labor, defines apostolic ambition.
Quoting Jeremiah 9:24: let the one who boasts boast in the Lord. All legitimate boasting finds its object in God, not self.
Self-commendation means nothing; only the Lord's commendation matters. Divine approval, not human self-promotion, establishes authenticity.
